glossary.def: A sequence of observations indexed by time, where the ordering carries information: neighbouring points are dependent, so the rows are not interchangeable the way i.i.d. data is. This dependence is what makes time series a distinct discipline.

The property that a series' mean, variance, and autocovariance do not change over time — the autocovariance depending only on the lag between points, never their absolute position. It is the substitute for i.i.d. that lets the past stand in for the future, and most forecasting assumes it holds after a transform.

The three parts of the classical decomposition y_t = T_t + S_t + R_t: the slow drift (trend), the fixed-period repeating pattern (seasonality), and the structureless leftover (remainder). A multiplicative variant y = T x S x R fits when seasonal swings scale with the level, and taking logs converts it to the additive form.

The correlation of a series with a lagged copy of itself, ρ_k = γ_k / γ_0, plotted against the lag k as a correlogram. Its shape is a fingerprint: geometric decay signals autoregressive memory, a sharp cut-off signals a moving-average process, and a tall spike at lag m signals seasonality of period m.

The correlation between y_t and y_{t-k} after removing the linear influence of all the lags in between — the direct dependence at range k, with relayed paths stripped out. Read alongside the ACF, it identifies the autoregressive order p, since the PACF of an AR(p) cuts off after lag p.

A sequence of uncorrelated, zero-mean, constant-variance shocks with every autocorrelation past lag 0 equal to zero. It is stationary and unforecastable beyond its mean, and it is the target for model residuals — white residuals mean all linear structure has been extracted.

A series y_t = y_{t-1} + ε_t formed by cumulating white noise; the AR(1) at its boundary φ = 1, a unit root. Its variance grows as t·σ², so it is non-stationary with no mean to revert to, and its first difference is exactly white noise — the canonical I(1) process.

Replacing a series with its step-to-step changes ∇y_t = y_t − y_{t-1} to remove a trend; the number of differences d needed to reach stationarity is the integration order, written I(d). It is the 'I' in ARIMA — but over-differencing injects artificial negative autocorrelation, so difference only as much as required.

A one-parameter family of power transforms, (y^λ − 1)/λ for λ ≠ 0 and ln y for λ = 0, used to stabilize a variance that grows with the level of the series. The log case (λ → 0) also turns a multiplicative seasonal pattern into an additive one.

refs.cite: Box, G. E. P., Jenkins, G. M., Reinsel, G. C. & Ljung, G. M. (2015). Time Series Analysis: Forecasting and Control (5th ed.). Wiley.
Hamilton, J. D. (1994). Time Series Analysis. Princeton University Press.
Hyndman, R. J. & Athanasopoulos, G. (2021). Forecasting: Principles and Practice (3rd ed.). OTexts.
Dickey, D. A. & Fuller, W. A. (1979). Distribution of the Estimators for Autoregressive Time Series with a Unit Root. JASA 74(366).
Box, G. E. P. & Cox, D. R. (1964). An Analysis of Transformations. J. R. Stat. Soc. B 26(2).
Ljung, G. M. & Box, G. E. P. (1978). On a Measure of Lack of Fit in Time Series Models. Biometrika 65(2).
Yule, G. U. (1927). On a Method of Investigating Periodicities in Disturbed Series. Phil. Trans. R. Soc. A 226.
